items.foco: Classification of primary vs secondary dysmenorrhea
items.title: Primary vs secondary dysmenorrhea
items.contentHtml:
Classification of primary vs secondary dysmenorrhea

  • Primary dysmenorrhea: menstrual pain without underlying pelvic pathology, appears in adolescents 6-12 months after menarche with ovulatory cycles; mediated by prostaglandins (PGF2α) that induce uterine contractions and ischemia.
  • Secondary dysmenorrhea: menstrual pain caused by organic pelvic disease, typically endometriosis, fibroids, adenomyosis or pelvic inflammatory disease; It usually begins years after menarche and may be accompanied by pain during sexual intercourse or abnormal bleeding.
  • Key difference: Primary responds to non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs and hormonal contraceptives; The secondary one requires treating the underlying cause and transvaginal ultrasound is the initial imaging study.

items.foco: NSAIDs as first line in dysmenorrhea
items.title: First line NSAIDs
items.contentHtml:
NSAIDs as first line in dysmenorrhea

  • Non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs are the first-line treatment for primary dysmenorrhea by inhibiting cyclooxygenase and reducing prostaglandin synthesis.
  • Ibuprofen 400-600 mg every 6-8 hours or naproxen 500 mg initially, then 250 mg each 6-8 hours, starting 1-2 days before the start of bleeding and continuing during the first 2-3 days of the cycle.
  • Evidence: non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs are superior to placebo in relieving pain; There is no significant difference between the different types of non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs in efficacy, but naproxen is preferred due to its longer half-life.
  • Trap: Do not use in patients with active peptic ulcer, kidney failure or allergy to aspirin; Paracetamol is not effective because it does not inhibit cyclooxygenase in uterine tissue.

items.title: Refractory hormonal contraceptives
items.contentHtml:
Hormonal contraceptives for refractory dysmenorrhea

  • When non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs are insufficient or contraindicated, combined hormonal contraceptives (ethinyl estradiol + progestin) are the second line of choice.
  • Mechanism: they suppress ovulation, stabilize the endometrium and reduce the production of prostaglandins; They are used in cycles of 21 active days and 7 placebo days, or in a continuous regimen to avoid withdrawal bleeding.
  • Vaginal ring or transdermal patch are alternatives with equal effectiveness; The levonorgestrel-releasing intrauterine device is also effective, especially in dysmenorrhea associated with endometriosis.
  • Trap: do not indicate combined hormonal contraceptives in migraine with aura, smokers over 35 years of age, uncontrolled hypertension or a history of venous thromboembolism.

items.foco: Endometriosis as a cause of secondary dysmenorrhea
items.title: Endometriosis secondary dysmenorrhea
items.contentHtml:
Endometriosis as a cause of secondary dysmenorrhea

  • Endometriosis is the most common cause of secondary dysmenorrhea, characterized by implants of endometrial tissue outside the uterus, which respond to estrogen and cause inflammation, fibrosis and cyclical pain.
  • The pain is usually progressive, begins before menstruation and persists afterwards; It is often associated with deep dyspareunia, dyschezia, and chronic pelvic pain.
  • Diagnosis: Transvaginal ultrasound can identify endometriotic cysts (endometriomas) and MRI is useful for deep disease; the gold standard is laparoscopy with histological confirmation.
  • Treatment: non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs, hormonal contraceptives, gonadotropin-releasing hormone agonists or conservative surgery; recurrence is common after stopping hormone therapy.

items.title: SPM criteria
items.contentHtml:
Diagnostic criteria for premenstrual syndrome (PMS)

  • The diagnosis of premenstrual syndrome requires at least one affective symptom (irritability, anxiety, depression, emotional lability) and one physical symptom (mastalgia, abdominal distension, edema, headache) that appear in the late luteal phase and disappear in the first days of menstruation.
  • Symptoms must be prospectively recorded for at least two cycles. consecutive, using scales such as the Daily Record of Severity of Problems (DRSP) to confirm the temporal relationship.
  • Exclusion criteria: the symptoms must not be a premenstrual exacerbation of an underlying psychiatric disorder (such as major depression or panic disorder); the absence of symptoms in the postmenstrual week is key.
  • Threshold: symptoms significantly interfere with daily life; If the interference is severe and affective symptoms predominate, it is considered premenstrual dysphoric disorder.

items.foco: Affective symptoms of PMS: irritability and depression
items.title: PMS affective symptoms
items.contentHtml:
Affective symptoms of PMS: irritability and depression

  • The most characteristic affective symptoms of premenstrual syndrome are irritability and depression, which tend to be the ones that most affect social and work functioning.
  • Irritability manifests itself as increased interpersonal sensitivity, outbursts of anger or conflicts; Depression includes sadness, hopelessness, anhedonia and fatigue.
  • These symptoms are triggered by the drop in estrogen and progesterone at the end of the luteal phase, which alters serotonergic, GABAergic and dopaminergic neurotransmission.
  • Trap: the presence of affective symptoms only in the luteal phase differentiates premenstrual syndrome from major depressive disorder, which persists throughout the cycle; Prospective registration is essential to avoid overdiagnosis.

items.title: PMDD differential diagnosis
items.contentHtml:
Premenstrual dysphoric disorder (PMDD): differential diagnosis

  • Premenstrual dysphoric disorder is a severe form of premenstrual syndrome, included in the DSM-5 as a depressive disorder, with at least 5 symptoms that include major affective symptoms (irritability, anxiety, depression, lability) and physical symptoms.
  • Criteria DSM-5:Symptoms must occur in most cycles over the past year, appear in the late luteal phase, and subside in the first few days of menstruation, with daily prospective confirmation.
  • Key differential diagnosis: Rule out major depressive disorder, bipolar disorder (especially rapid cycling), generalized anxiety disorder, and panic disorder; premenstrual exacerbation of these disorders does not meet criteria for premenstrual dysphoric disorder.
  • Tool: the 14-item Daily Record of Severity of Problems (DRSP) is the standard for documenting cyclicity and severity.

items.foco: Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors in PMDD
items.title: SSRIs in PMDD
items.contentHtml:
Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors in PMDD

  • Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors are the first-line treatment for premenstrual dysphoric disorder, with proven efficacy for affective symptoms such as irritability, depression and anxiety.
  • Dose and regimen: fluoxetine 20 mg/day, sertraline 50-150 mg/day or paroxetine 20 mg/day; They can be administered continuously or only in the luteal phase (14 days before menstruation), with equal effectiveness.
  • Mechanism: Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors act by modulating serotonergic neurotransmission, which is sensitive to hormonal fluctuations in the luteal phase; The response is usually rapid (days) unlike major depression.
  • Trap: Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors are not effective for the physical symptoms of PMS (mastalgia, bloating); For these, diuretics or spironolactone are used.

items.foco: Supplements with calcium and vitamin B6 in SPM
items.title: Calcium B6 SPM supplements
items.contentHtml:
Supplements with calcium and vitamin B6 in PMS

  • Calcium (1200 mg/day) is the supplement with the most evidence to reduce symptoms of premenstrual syndrome, both emotional and physical; Its mechanism includes mood stabilization and reduction of water retention.
  • Vitamin B6 (50-100 mg/day) as a cofactor in serotonin synthesis can improve mild affective symptoms, although the evidence is limited and high doses (>200 mg/day) are associated with peripheral neuropathy.
  • Additional options: magnesium (200-400 mg/day) for mastalgia and distension, and vitamin E (400 IU/day) for physical symptoms; the combination of calcium and vitamin D can enhance the benefit.
  • Trap: supplements are useful as adjuvants, but do not replace pharmacological treatment in moderate to severe cases; always evaluate previous dietary intake to avoid toxicity.

items.foco: Exercise and cognitive behavioral therapy for PMS
items.title: CBT SPM Exercise
items.contentHtml:
Exercise and cognitive-behavioral therapy for PMS

  • Aerobic exercise (30-60 minutes, 3-5 times a week) reduces emotional and physical symptoms of PMS by releasing endorphins, improving mood and reducing water retention.
  • Cognitive-behavioral therapy helps manage irritability, anxiety and negative thinking patterns associated with premenstrual syndrome, with sustained long-term effects.
  • Both interventions are considered first-line in the non-pharmacological management of mild to moderate premenstrual syndrome, and can be combined with supplements or drugs.
  • Trap: intense or excessive exercise can worsen symptoms in some women; Moderate and regular activity is recommended, avoiding overtraining.

items.foco: Uterine fibroids as a cause of secondary dysmenorrhea
items.title: Fibroids secondary dysmenorrhea
items.contentHtml:
Uterine fibroids as a cause of secondary dysmenorrhea

  • Uterine fibroids (leiomyomas) are benign smooth muscle tumors that can cause secondary dysmenorrhea, especially submucosal and intramural tumors, by distorting the endometrial cavity and increasing uterine contractions.
  • The pain is usually cramping, accompanied by heavy menstrual bleeding (menorrhagia) and a sensation of pressure. pelvic; secondary dysmenorrhea due to fibroids is more common in women over 30 years of age.
  • Diagnosis: transvaginal ultrasound is the initial study, identifying the location, size and number of fibroids; hysterosonography or magnetic resonance imaging are useful for submucosal fibroids.
  • Treatment: non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs, hormonal contraceptives, levonorgestrel-releasing intrauterine device to reduce bleeding; Myomectomy or uterine artery embolization is reserved for symptomatic cases that do not respond to medical management.

items.foco: Spironolactone for physical symptoms of PMS
items.title: Spironolactone SPM physical
items.contentHtml:
Spironolactone for physical symptoms of PMS

  • Spironolactone is a potassium-sparing diuretic and aldosterone antagonist that is used for physical symptoms of premenstrual syndrome such as abdominal bloating, edema and mastalgia, by reducing water retention.
  • Dose: 25-100 mg/day, administered during the luteal phase (from day 14 of the cycle until the beginning of menstruation); Continuous use is not recommended due to the risk of hyperkalemia.
  • Mechanism: Spironolactone blocks aldosterone receptors, counteracting the sodium and water retention effect induced by progesterone in the luteal phase.
  • Trap: Spironolactone is not effective for affective symptoms of premenstrual syndrome; In these cases, selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors are used. Monitor serum potassium in patients with renal failure or use of angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitors.
